title: Witch Week - Chapter 3 (Fragment 2)
text:
“I thought we would have a change today,” she said, when everyone was settled. “Our poetry book doesn’t seem to be going down very well, does it?” She looked brightly round the class; 6B looked back cautiously. Some of them felt anything would be better than being asked to find poems beautiful. Some of them felt it depended on what Miss Hodge intended to do instead. Of the rest, Nan was trying not to cry, Brian was licking a scratch on his arm, and Charles was glowering. Charles liked poetry because the lines were so short. You could think your own thoughts in the spaces around the print.

“Today,” said Miss Hodge, “I want you all to do something yourselves.”

Everyone recoiled. Estelle put her hand up. “Please, Miss Hodge. I don’t know how to write poems.”

“Oh, I don’t want you to do that,” said Miss Hodge. Everyone relaxed. “I want you to act out some little plays for me.” Everyone recoiled again. Miss Hodge took no notice and explained that she was going to call them out to the front in pairs, a boy and a girl in each, and every pair was going to act out the same short scene. “That way,” she said, “we shall have fifteen different pocket dramas.” By this time, most of 6B were staring at her in wordless despair. Miss Hodge smiled around them and prepared to galvanize them. Really, she thought, her scheme might go quite well after all. “Now, we must choose a subject for our playlets. It has to be something strong and striking, with passionate possibilities. Suppose we act a pair of lovers saying good-bye?” Somebody groaned, as Miss Hodge had known somebody would. “Very well. Who has a suggestion?”

Theresa’s hand was up, and Dan Smith’s.

“A television star and her admirer,” said Theresa.

“A murderer and a policeman making him confess,” said Dan. “Are we allowed to torture?”

“No, we are not,” said Miss Hodge, at which Dan lost interest. “Anyone else?”

Nirupam raised a long thin arm. “A salesman deceiving a lady over a car.”

Well, Miss Hodge thought, she had not really expected anyone to make a suggestion that would give them away. She pretended to consider. “We-ell, so far the most dramatic suggestion is Dan’s. But I had in mind something really tense, which we all know about quite well.”

“We all know about murder,” Dan protested.

“Yes,” said Miss Hodge. She was watching everyone like a hawk now. “But we know even more about stealing, say, or lying, or witchcraft, or—” She let herself notice the broomstick again, with a start of surprise. It came in handy after all. “I know! Let us suppose that one of the people in our little play is suspected of being a witch, and the other is an inquisitor. How about that?”

Nothing. Not a soul in 6B reacted, except Dan. “That’s the same as my idea,” he grumbled. “And it’s no fun without torture.”

Miss Hodge made Dan into suspect number one at once. “Then you begin, Dan,” she said, “with Theresa. Which are you, Theresa—witch or inquisitor?”

“Inquisitor, Miss Hodge,” Theresa said promptly.

“It’s not fair!” said Dan. “I don’t know what witches do!”

Nor did he, it was clear. And it was equally clear that Theresa had no more idea what inquisitors did. They stood woodenly by the blackboard. Dan stared at the ceiling, while Theresa stated, “You are a witch.” Whereupon Dan told the ceiling, “No I am not.” And they went on doing this until Miss Hodge told them to stop. Regretfully, she demoted Dan from first suspect to last, and put Theresa down there with him, and called up the next pair.

vocabulary.definition: To perform or dramatize a scene or role.
vocabulary.example: “I want you to act out some little plays for me.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows enthusiasm or happiness; vividly.
vocabulary.example: ” She looked brightly round the class; 6B looked back cautiously.

vocabulary.definition: In a careful manner to avoid danger or mistakes.
vocabulary.example: ” She looked brightly round the class; 6B looked back cautiously.

vocabulary.definition: To admit or state that one has done something wrong.
vocabulary.example: “A murderer and a policeman making him confess,” said Dan.

vocabulary.definition: To cause someone to believe something that is not true.
vocabulary.example: “A salesman deceiving a lady over a car.

vocabulary.definition: To lower to a less important position or rank.
vocabulary.example: Regretfully, she demoted Dan from first suspect to last, and put Theresa down there with him, and called up the next pair.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to drama or sudden and striking events.
vocabulary.example: “We-ell, so far the most dramatic suggestion is Dan’s.

vocabulary.definition: To shock or excite someone into taking action.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge smiled around them and prepared to galvanize them.

vocabulary.definition: Looking angry or sullenly at someone or something.
vocabulary.example: Of the rest, Nan was trying not to cry, Brian was licking a scratch on his arm, and Charles was glowering.

vocabulary.definition: To make a deep sound expressing pain, disappointment, or dissatisfaction.
vocabulary.example: ” Somebody groaned, as Miss Hodge had known somebody would.

vocabulary.definition: A person who asks questions aggressively, especially in historical religious context.
vocabulary.example: Let us suppose that one of the people in our little play is suspected of being a witch, and the other is an inquisitor.

vocabulary.definition: A person who unlawfully kills another.
vocabulary.example: “A murderer and a policeman making him confess,” said Dan.

vocabulary.definition: Two people or things that are joined or considered together.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge took no notice and explained that she was going to call them out to the front in pairs, a boy and a girl in each, and every pair was going to act out the same short scene.

vocabulary.definition: Showing or caused by strong feelings or beliefs.
vocabulary.example: It has to be something strong and striking, with passionate possibilities.

vocabulary.definition: Brief, concise dramatic performances or scenes.
vocabulary.example: “That way,” she said, “we shall have fifteen different pocket dramas.

vocabulary.definition: Literary work in verse form, expressing ideas and emotions with rhythm and style.
vocabulary.example: “Our poetry book doesn’t seem to be going down very well, does it?

vocabulary.definition: Without delay; immediately.
vocabulary.example:
”

“Inquisitor, Miss Hodge,” Theresa said promptly.

vocabulary.definition: To suddenly spring or flinch back in fear, horror, or disgust.
vocabulary.example:
”

Everyone recoiled.

vocabulary.definition: To become less tense or anxious; to rest.
vocabulary.example: Everyone relaxed.

vocabulary.definition: A plan or program of action, especially a secret or complicated one.
vocabulary.example: Really, she thought, her scheme might go quite well after all.

vocabulary.definition: Gaps or intervals between objects or elements.
vocabulary.example: You could think your own thoughts in the spaces around the print.

vocabulary.definition: Attracting attention by being unusual or remarkable.
vocabulary.example: It has to be something strong and striking, with passionate possibilities.

vocabulary.definition: Believed to be possibly guilty or involved in something wrong.
vocabulary.example: Let us suppose that one of the people in our little play is suspected of being a witch, and the other is an inquisitor.

vocabulary.definition: Showing nervousness or strain.
vocabulary.example: But I had in mind something really tense, which we all know about quite well.

vocabulary.definition: The act of causing severe physical or mental pain.
vocabulary.example: “Are we allowed to torture?

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic, especially black magic.
vocabulary.example: “But we know even more about stealing, say, or lying, or witchcraft, or—” She let herself notice the broomstick again, with a start of surprise.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of hopelessness or sadness expressed without words.
vocabulary.example: ” By this time, most of 6B were staring at her in wordless despair.
